creator: mandree@FreeBSD.org
text:
Oh come on. We are not talking about Python, but flex is about C or C++, so whitespace DOES NOT MATTER. 

If you compile generated code with -Werror and turn on formatting warnings, that is the problem.  So stop doing stupid things.

If you want flex fixed, talk to the upstream maintainers and see how you can help there, such that the onus is not on the world and its brother and dozens of distributors need to fix things.

I concede that OpenBSD's patch is more or less bite-sized and I could easily use it, but I also do know some downstream software packages that patch flex-generated code because flex templates used to have grave bugs, and changing the template/skeleton code and throwing off line numbers and context by importing patches would break those.   That is a use case I support, but not "make stuff the actual compiler stage does not see an error".  So no, not taking OpenBSD's patch either.

So either you are developing your own code, then you can remove the -Werror for all third-party code that troubles you, or you add -Wno-misleading-indentation and other -Wno-other-stupid-warning.

There is a difference between developer/maintainer warnings and making the compiler complain about REAL issues, such as time_t width.

(Did I mention that some reasonable Windows 10 code will not compile with VS2017 in stricts conformance mode with warning level 4 and the cl.exe equivalent of -Werror, /wx?, because, well, winnt.h contains noncompliant code?)
